data.title: Birkenstock Stays Step Ahead with Dresden Acquisition — Market Talk
data.description: Birkenstock scales its infrastructure ahead of demand with its opportunistic purchase of a large-scale production facility near Dresden, Jefferies analysts say. The 18 million-euro deal, enabled by the seller's bankruptcy, fast tracks its expansion "at a compelling 240 euros per square meter." The site includes 78,000 square meters of production and logistics space, plus 80,000 square meters of undeveloped land. Likely up and running by fiscal 2027, the site supports growth for the shoemaker's sandals, clogs and footbeds. Birkenstock also says that sales will be higher than expected after the company raised prices in response to the Trump administration's tariffs on European imports to the U.S. Shares fall 2.2%. (emon.reiser@wsj.com )

data.title: British Pound Hits 7-week Low
data.description:
GBPUSD decreased to 1.33, the lowest since August 2025.

Over the past 4 weeks, British Pound US Dollar lost 1.06%, and in the last 12 months, it decreased 0.57%.

data.title: Should Apple buy Intel's stock? These analysts suggest a better investment.
data.description:
By Emily Bary

Intel reportedly approached Apple about an investment, but absent 'obvious benefits' to the smartphone giant, some analysts say the company would be better off just repurchasing more of its own stock

Apple, led by Tim Cook, could spend upwards of $90 billion on buybacks this fiscal year. Bernstein analysts say those are a better use of cash than a hypothetical stake in Intel.

Intel Corp. (INTC) reportedly has had early-stage talks about a potential investment from Apple Inc. But analysts at Bernstein see a better use for that hypothetical cash.

"Without obvious benefits to Apple ... we would far prefer the company to buy back its own shares vs buying [Intel] shares," Bernstein analysts said in a Thursday note to clients.

Apple (AAPL) could spend $92 billion on buybacks this fiscal year, according to FactSet estimates, after having spent $95 billion last fiscal year. The company boosts its buyback authorization annually at levels that are the highest in the market. Based on data from Birinyi Associates from late August, which looked at all U.S. companies, Apple occupied the top six spots for annual buyback announcements and shared the No. 7 spot.

Companies buy back their shares as a means of returning capital to shareholders and lowering the share count, which in turn can boost earnings per share. Buybacks can also suggest to investors that executives see a company's stock as undervalued at current levels.

Read: Will Apple deliver Intel its next lifeline? Here's why analysts think a deal would make sense.

It's also worth noting that Nvidia Corp. (NVDA) agreed to invest $5 billion in Intel last week, though that amount pales in comparison to the latest $60 billion boost to Nvidia's buyback authorization that was announced in August.

See also: Nvidia has $57 billion in cash. Wall Street has ideas about what to do with it.

After Bloomberg News reported late Wednesday that Intel reached out to Apple about a possible investment as well as a closer relationship, the Bernstein team acknowledged that a stake was at least "plausible." Apple "is large, has plenty of cash, and (in theory) could use alternative sources of leading-edge manufacturing capacity." Intel, meanwhile, has been lining up investors - first with the U.S. government and then with Nvidia Corp.

But money doesn't seem to be the biggest issue for Intel, according to the Bernstein team. Intel isn't "in any imminent cash crunch" and rather needs better technological innovation to win over customers who can fill the manufacturing capacity it'll likely need to boost to satisfy the Trump administration.

Intel has struggled with technological missteps over the years and even if the government tries to induce U.S. companies to use Intel's chip-fabrication capabilities, the Bernstein team struggles to see how the company could attract "meaningful volume" without dramatically improving its capabilities.

"Thus we continue to wonder why companies have been so eager to throw money Intel's way given their track record; we suspect another sort of encouragement is likely happening here," the analysts said. They later noted that an investment could provide "possible further ingratiation with the current U.S. administration," which admittedly "in itself could make this deal worth it if it continues to avoid worst case scenarios for tariffs."

-Emily Bary

This content was created by MarketWatch, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. MarketWatch is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal.

data.title: My City Builders, Inc. SEC 10-Q Report
data.description:
My City Builders, Inc., a company specializing in real estate development and rental income, has released its Form 10-Q report for the second quarter of 2025. The report highlights substantial growth in revenue and a significant reduction in net loss, reflecting the company's strategic focus on expanding its rental income and real estate transactions.

Financial Highlights

Total Revenue: $90.232 million, increased from $35.645 million in the prior year, reflecting growth in rental income.

Net Loss: $215.051 million, a significant reduction from $1.174 million in the previous year, primarily due to decreased other expenses and increased revenue.

Basic and diluted loss per share of common stock: $(0.02), improved from $(0.24) in the previous year, indicating a reduction in net loss per share.

Business Highlights

Revenue Segments: The company generated rental income from operating leases, which is accounted for under ASC 842. Operating lease revenue is generally recognized on a straight-line basis over the terms of the lease agreements.

Geographical Performance: The company, through its subsidiary RAC, focuses on real estate transactions in Gadsden, Alabama, and Laurel, Mississippi, with plans to build low-income single-family homes and duplex apartments.

Sales Units: As of April 30, 2025, RAC had twelve completed homes with three additional homes under construction in East Gadsden. Out of the twelve completed properties, nine homes have been leased.

New Production Launches: The company acquired land in Gadsden, Alabama, for the construction of new residential homes, with plans to build up to thirty single-family garden homes as a 'Phase Two' construction project.

Future Outlook: The company plans to continue its focus on real estate transactions, particularly in low-income housing, and aims to raise necessary funding through equity and debt financing arrangements to support its operations and business plan development.

SEC Filing: My City Builders, Inc. [ MYCB ] - 10-Q - Sep. 25, 2025

data.title: London Mining Week Grows in Scale and Influence
data.description:
London, United Kingdom--(Newsfile Corp. - September 25, 2025) - Since launching in June this year, London Mining Week has quickly established itself as one of the most important weeks in the global mining calendar. From 30 November to 5 December, London will host a high-impact programme of events bringing together industry leaders, investors, policymakers, and innovators from across the mining sector.

Curated by Beacon Events and anchored by the Resourcing Tomorrow conference, the week unites the full value chain through conferences, roundtables, networking receptions, and deal-making opportunities across the capital. From Mayfair to the Square Mile, London Mining Week reflects the momentum of an industry critical to the global economy and the minerals powering our future.

With its unrivalled concentration of financial institutions, legal expertise, and international mining leadership, London offers the ideal platform for dialogue, investment, and cross-border collaboration. Whether the focus is capital markets, policy, technology, or ESG, London is where the future of mining is shaped.

At the centre of the week is Resourcing Tomorrow (2-4 December), a world-class conference drawing 2,000+ senior decision-makers from across the mining ecosystem which serves as the driving force behind London Mining Week's expanding programme.

Nick Rastall, Event Director of Resourcing Tomorrow, said:

"We're delighted to see London Mining Week grow into such a landmark week for the mining industry. For five days, leading players from across the value chain will come together to connect, exchange ideas, and drive the sector forward."

London Mining Week will showcase an extensive programme of flagship conferences, including Resourcing Tomorrow, the International Metals Symposium, the Critical Minerals Association's 5th Annual Conference, and MINEX Eurasia.

Complementing these are exclusive roundtables and networking events such as the invite-only Resourcing Tomorrow Government Roundtable, the Oxford Mining Club Winter Drinks, Women in Mining Drinks, Young Mining Professionals Drinks, and Mining4Beer, which will bring together industry leaders and peers.

The week will culminate in major celebrations, with the Resourcing Tomorrow Outstanding Achievement Awards & Gala Dinner and the Battery Britain Gala & Awards set to be standout highlights.

London Mining Week brings together the full mining ecosystem-from governments and investors to majors, juniors, and next-gen talent-for a week of insight, deal-making, and collaboration across the capital.

View the full programme and register your interest at mining.london

About Beacon Events

About Advanced Medical Solutions Group plc - see www.admedsol.com

AMS is a world-leading independent developer and manufacturer of innovative tissue-healing technology, focused on quality outcomes for patients and value for payers. AMS has a wide range of surgical products including tissue adhesives, sutures, haemostats, internal fixation devices and internal sealants, which it markets under its brands LiquiBand®, RESORBA®, LiquiBandFix8®, LIQUIFIX™, Peters Surgical, Ifabond, Vitalitec and Seal-G®. AMS also supplies wound care dressings such as silver alginates, alginates and foams through its ActivHeal® brand as well as under white label. Since 2019, the Group has made seven acquisitions: Sealantis, an Israeli developer of innovative internal sealants, Biomatlante, a French developer and manufacturer of surgical biomaterials, Raleigh, a leading UK coater and converter of woundcare and bio-diagnostics materials, AFS Medical, an Austrian specialist surgical business, Connexicon, an Irish tissue adhesives specialist, Syntacoll, a German specialist in collagen-based absorbable surgical implants and Peters Surgical, a global provider of specialty surgical sutures, mechanical haemostasis and internal cyanoacrylate devices.

AMS's products, manufactured in the UK, Germany, France, the Netherlands, Thailand, India, the Czech Republic and Israel, are sold globally via a network of multinational or regional partners and distributors, as well as via AMS's own direct sales forces in the UK, Germany, Austria, France, Poland, Benelux, India, the Czech Republic and Russia. The Group has R&D innovation hubs in the UK, Ireland, Germany, France and Israel. Established in 1991, the Group has more than 1,600 employee For more information, please see www.admedsol.com.

data.title: Flotilla for Gaza poses no threat to Israel, Spanish minister says
data.description:
The Global Sumud Flotilla heading to Gaza protected by a Spanish military vessel poses no threat to anyone, including Israel, Spain's Foreign Minister Jose Manuel Albares told Reuters on Thursday.

In an interview, Albares added that Spain had accepted Belgium's request to assist Belgian citizens on board the flotilla if needed, and was holding conversations with Ireland on the same subject.

"The mandate of the mission is very clear. We are having many incidents with this flotilla and this ship is there to provide assistance to the Spanish, now also to the Belgian citizens and we are talking with other European colleagues enquiring as to whether we can do the same thing," he said.

He added that the mandate of the aid flotilla was also clear: "We are talking about a peaceful humanitarian flotilla ... that is no threat to anyone, no threat for Israel."

Its single objective is "that humanitarian assistance can be accessed by the people of Gaza," he added.

"I don't see why anyone would object or want to harm them," Albares said.

data.title: Ecuador prison riot leaves at least 17 dead
data.description:
At least 17 people were killed in a prison riot in Ecuador's northern city of Esmeraldas, near the border with Colombia, the country's prison agency said on Thursday.

Authorities are carrying out the corresponding investigations to clarify what happened, the agency added.

The riot follows another deadly outbreak earlier this week in a prison in southern Ecuador, where 14 people were killed.

Ecuador has faced a wave of prison violence in recent years, with hundreds of inmates killed.

President Daniel Noboa's government, which has pledged to crack down on crime, blames the unrest on gangs competing for territory and control inside the prison system.

data.title: Ex-Fed and Treasury chiefs urge Supreme Court not to let Trump fire Cook
data.description:
Key points:
  • Trump is asking Supreme Court to let him remove Cook
  • Filing says Cook firing threatens Fed independence
  • Trump makes unproven mortgage fraud allegations

By Dan Burns

A group of 18 former U.S. Federal Reserve officials, Treasury secretaries and other top economic officials who served under presidents from both parties urged the Supreme Court on Thursday to reject President Donald Trump's petition to allow his unprecedented attempt to fire Fed Governor Lisa Cook to stand.

The group included the past three Fed chairs - Janet Yellen, Ben Bernanke and Alan Greenspan - as well as former Treasury secretaries Henry Paulson, Lawrence Summers, Jacob Lew, Timothy Geithner and Robert Rubin. In a brief to the Supreme Court, they argued that letting the Republican president remove Cook while her legal challenge to Trump's action is ongoing would threaten the U.S. central bank's independence and erode public confidence in it.

Both a district court judge and an appellate court - by a 2-1 vote - have rejected Trump so far.

Trump's administration asked the top U.S. judicial body on September 18 to let him move ahead with firing Cook, an appointee of his Democratic predecessor Joe Biden, in a move without precedent since the Fed's founding in 1913. The Justice Department asked the Supreme Court to lift U.S. District Judge Jia Cobb's order temporarily blocking Trump's action.

In the amicus - or "friend-of-the-court" - brief, the group wrote that "the balance of equitable factors, and in particular consideration of potential harms to the public, weighs heavily in favor of denying the stay application because premature removal of a Governor would undermine the public's confidence in the independence of the Federal Reserve."

"The risk of harm to the Federal Reserve's longstanding reputation for independence - and the ensuing harm to the economy - more than justifies maintaining the status quo by keeping Governor Cook in her position while the legality of her removal is adjudicated," they added.

The filing came hours before Cook's response is due to Trump's request to the Supreme Court.

Cobb ruled that Trump likely lacked sufficient cause to fire her over so-far-unproven allegations of mortgage fraud before she took office - allegations she denies - related to properties she owns in Georgia and Michigan, under the law that created the Fed. A federal circuit court later reasoned that Trump fired her without sufficient due process but did not address the question of cause.

Members of the Fed board are appointed by a president and confirmed by the Senate, but the Federal Reserve Act of 1913, as amended, states that they can only be removed by a president "for cause." No president before Trump, a persistent critic of the Fed and its leadership who regularly demands a large cut in interest rates, has ever tried to remove a Fed governor.

Trump last month announced he was firing Cook over allegations made by his director of the Federal Housing Finance Agency, William Pulte, that she improperly listed her properties in Georgia and Michigan both as her primary residence in obtaining refinanced loans in 2021 before she took office.

No charges have so far been filed associated with it, and Reuters reported that Cook had in other documents associated with her mortgage application declared her Georgia property as a vacation home, potentially undercutting Pulte's accusation.

The amicus brief filers do not address the accusations at the center of Trump's effort to fire her, instead focusing on the legislative history of the Fed's creation to argue it was the explicit intent of Congress to insulate the central bank from political pressures and that allowing the firing would lead to dire economic consequences, especially higher inflation.

"Any reduction in the Federal Reserve's well-earned reputation of independence would hamper its ability to influence independent decisionmakers and could ultimately lead to greater inflation," they wrote. "The same outcome will follow from actions that appear to politicize its membership or erode its ability to engage in monetary policymaking free from political influence."

data.title: US urges utilities to keep coal-fired plants running as AI demand booms, energy secretary says
data.description:
Key points:
  • US plans to push back retirement of many more coal power plants
  • Plan to boost power includes more coal, nuclear and backup generation
  • US needs 100 GW more firm capacity in five years, excluding renewables
  • DOE receives 300 inquiries for new power plant, data center sites

By Laila Kearney and Timothy Gardner

The administration of U.S. President Donald Trump is urging utilities across the country to keep coal-fired power plants running longer, in part to help deliver the vast amount of electricity needed to fuel artificial intelligence, Energy Secretary Chris Wright told Reuters on Thursday.

Delaying the retirement of often half-century-old coal plants is part of a broader strategy to increase the country's power output that will also include boosting nuclear energy and allowing backup power plants to operate around the clock.

The administration has made expanding energy production a top priority while rejecting concerns about climate change, which Trump told the United Nations this week amounted to a global "con job."

"We've got to speed up firm capacity and stop retirement of firm capacity," Wright said, adding the government had been in discussions with lots of utilities nationwide and expects the majority of the several dozen U.S. coal plants nearing retirement to delay closure.

"Utilities across the country are saying, thank you," Wright said. "We don't want to close them."

Wright said the U.S. would also aim to get more out of the existing grid by operating backup generators and peaker plants, which typically ramp up temporarily to meet spiking power use, more permanently.

The White House is also seeking to boost nuclear energy, including through regulatory reforms to speed permitting and hosting new nuclear technologies through the Department of Energy.

"We need that industry as another source of energy, and so we're going to give temporary nudges to get it started," Wright said.

Currently, two shut U.S. nuclear power plants - including one on Three Mile Island in Pennsylvania - are in the unprecedented process of being restarted. Three Mile Island, dubbed the Crane Clean Energy Center, would deliver electricity for Microsoft data centers.

Total U.S. electricity demand is projected to hit record highs this year and next, according to the Energy Information Administration. Growth in the country's power consumption will also continue to accelerate through the end of the decade as new massive AI data center campuses power up.

Wright said the U.S. will need 100 gigawatts more of firm capacity in the next five years. That would likely not include renewables like solar and wind power which do not typically run around the clock, he said. A gigawatt is enough power for about 1 million U.S. homes.

The Department of Energy, this year, also opened its own land for the development of power plants and data centers. So far, the department has received some 300 inquiries, Wright said.

data.title: General Dynamics Unit Gets $1.5 Billion Contract to Boost U.S. Strategic Command's IT Capabilities
data.description:
By Kelly Cloonan

General Dynamics said its information-technology unit was awarded a $1.5 billion contract from the U.S. Strategic Command to modernize its IT capabilities.

The aerospace and defense company said Thursday the contract was awarded in May and covers a one-year base period with options for six additional years.

Under the contract, General Dynamics Information Technology will use its digital engineering capabilities to cut STRATCOM's costs, boost efficiency and improve collaboration among mission partners.

The unit will also integrate artificial-intelligence technologies into STRATCOM's enterprise data, and transition STRATCOM to a new hybrid cloud environment for greater flexibility and scalability. In addition, the unit will implement cybersecurity solutions, the Reston, Va., company said.

STRATCOM oversees strategic deterrence, global strike nuclear command and control, and electromagnetic-spectrum operations.

Write to Kelly Cloonan at kelly.cloonan@wsj.com

data.title: Nestle Names New Nespresso CEO After Navratil Steps In to Lead Group — Update
data.description:
By Adam Whittaker and Mauro Orru

Nestle named Alfonso Gonzalez Loeschen as chief executive of Nespresso, turning to a company veteran to lead its coffee business after former head Philipp Navratil was appointed CEO of the group.

The Swiss maker of Nescafe coffee and Purina pet food said Gonzalez Loeschen, currently CEO of Nespresso's North American business, would assume the position as global head of the brand on Nov. 1. He will also sit on the group's executive board.

His career at Nestle dates back to 1992, when he was an assistant marketing manager in Mexico. He went on to fill several leadership roles in Mexico, Puerto Rico and the U.S. Gonzalez Loeschen has been CEO of Nespresso's North American business, covering the U.S., Canada and Mexico, since January 2020.

His elevation to global head of Nespresso comes weeks after the company appointed Philipp Navratil, who previously had that role, as Nestle chief executive to replace Laurent Freixe.

Nestle dismissed Freixe earlier this month after it said it conducted an investigation into an undisclosed romantic relationship between Freixe and a direct subordinate that breached the group's code of conduct.

Last week, the company said Chairman Paul Bulcke, who oversaw that investigation, would step down after nearly a decade at the helm of the board.

Freixe's abrupt ouster was the latest dramatic shift in the top echelons of Nestle in a little more than a year. Nestle had appointed the French executive in 2024 to replace CEO Mark Schneider in a bid to revive growth as the company grappled with sluggish sales.

In July, the company said it was launching a review of underperforming brands in its vitamins, minerals and supplements unit that might result in a sale, as it seeks to steer the business toward higher-end products such as Garden of Life and Solgar supplements. Nestle reported organic sales growth of 3% for the second quarter.

Write to Adam Whittaker at adam.whittaker@wsj.com and Mauro Orru at mauro.orru@wsj.com

data.title: India proposes tweaks to foreign investment rules for exports in possible win for Amazon
data.description:
Key points:
  • Amazon lobbying for changes to India's foreign investment rules
  • India drafts proposal to only relax rules for exports
  • Amazon argues move can help small sellers ramp up exports
  • Small brick-and-mortar retailers oppose change

By Aditya Kalra

India's government has drafted a proposal to ease foreign investment rules to allow e-commerce companies like Amazon to buy products directly from Indian sellers and then sell them to overseas customers, a document showed.

India currently prohibits foreign e-commerce companies from selling goods directly to consumers either at home or abroad, allowing them only to operate a marketplace to connect buyers and sellers for a fee.

The policy has been a sore point between New Delhi and Washington for years, and Amazon has been lobbying the Indian government to ease the rules in the case of exports, Reuters has reported.

The proposed changes come as India and the U.S. struggle to iron out differences over a long-delayed trade deal, and despite groups backing millions of small Indian brick-and-mortar retailers demanding the government shun Amazon's request. They say the U.S. company's financial firepower threatens their businesses.

Less than 10% of small Indian businesses selling online domestically participate in global e-commerce exports, "constrained by complex documentation, compliance requirements", said a 10-page proposal from The Directorate General of Foreign Trade (DGFT), which is not public but was seen by Reuters on Thursday.

"The proposal envisages a third-party export facilitation model, wherein a dedicated export entity linked to e-commerce platforms would manage compliance."

The DGFT and Amazon did not respond to Reuters queries. The proposal will require a sign-off from India's cabinet.

Amazon said in December it helped to generate $13 billion in cumulative exports for sellers from India since 2015, and plans to increase that to $80 billion by 2030.

The DGFT draft said the relaxed rules would only apply to exports, and any breaches of the policy will attract stiff penalties along with criminal action.

Last year, the Indian antitrust watchdog found that Amazon breached competition laws by giving preference to select sellers, allegations it denies.

data.title: KalVista Pharmaceuticals Shares Drop After Notes Offering Prices
data.description:
By Chris Wack

KalVista Pharmaceuticals shares were 9% lower, at $11.83, after the company priced its offering of $125 million of 3.25% convertible senior notes due 2031 in a private placement.

The company said the offering was increased from the previously announced offering size of $110 million.

KalVista also granted the initial purchasers of the notes an option to buy, for settlement within a 13-day period from, and including, the date on which the notes are first issued, up to an additional $18.75 million of notes.

The sale of the notes is expected to close Monday.

The notes will be senior, unsecured obligations of KalVista, and will bear interest at a rate of 3.25% per year, payable semi-annually in arrears on April 1 and Oct. 1 of each year, beginning on Oct. 1, 2026. The notes will mature on Oct. 1, 2031, unless earlier converted, repurchased or redeemed in accordance with the terms of the notes.

KalVista estimates the net proceeds from the offering will be $120.8 million, or $139 million if the initial purchasers exercise their option to purchase additional notes in full.

KalVista expects to use the proceeds from the offering for working capital and other general corporate purposes.

Write to Chris Wack at chris.wack@wsj.com
